coredata.dc:title: Efficient detection of Picric acid by pyranone based Schiff base as a chemosensor
coredata.dc:creator: Goel, Apurva
Malhotra, Rajesh
coredata.dc:description: Keeping in mind the necessity to prepare a budget friendly chemosensor for the effortless and convenient sensing of one of the explosives i.e., Picric acid, a Schiff base 3-[-1-(4-fluoro-phenylimino)-ethyl]-4‑hydroxy-6-methyl-pyran-2-one (DFA) was synthesized by the condensation reaction between dehydroacetic and 4-fluoroaniline in ethanol solvent and characterized through analytical spectral techniques. The sensing capability of Schiff base for Picric acid as compared to other nitro explosives was examined by means of UV–Vis absorbance studies. A considerable band shift in Schiff base DFA′s absorbance band for Picric acid while irrelevant changes for the rest of explosives showed DFA′s selectivity towards Picric acid. The binding stoichiometric ratio was found to be 1:1 for DFA: Picric acid adducts which was deliberated from the job′s plot and confirmed by 1H NMR titration. The Association constant and Limit of detection were acquired to be 1.3 × 105 M−1 and 6.92 × 10−7 M respectively using Benesi-Hildebrand (B-H) equation. The sensing power of Schiff base DFA showed high selectivity and sensitivity as addition of competitive explosives showed no alteration in its detection towards Picric acid also the applicability at almost every pH and stability over an extensive time period showed DFA supremacy for Picric acid detection. DFT calculations were carried for the confirmation of theoretical structural relationships and actual absorption data. Aggregation study were too conducted in THF /water Schiff base solution and a maximum in THF /70% water solution showed aggregation at its utmost in Schiff base at 70% water solution and this was further confirmed using FESEM images.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Schiff base DFA
Picric acid
UV-Vis absorbance studies
LOD
Aggregation phenomenon
DFT calculations

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Designing and synthesis of Schiff base DFA
Evaluation of stiochiometric ratio: Jobs plot and association constant Ka: B-H equation
Competitive experimentation for validation of effectiveness of Schiff base
pH effect on Picric acid detection
Time of response and optical stability

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Council of Scientific and Industrial Research, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Apurva Goel is pleased to CSIR, New Delhi, for pecuniary assistance in terms of JRF fellowship with CSIR sanction letter No. 09/752(0070)/2017-EMR-I. The authors are grateful to the Central Instrumentation Lab (CIL), Guru Jambheshwar University of Science and Technology, Hisar for their instrumental support.

Apurva Goel is pleased to CSIR , New Delhi, for pecuniary assistance in terms of JRF fellowship with CSIR sanction letter No. 09/752(0070)/2017-EMR-I . The authors are grateful to the Central Instrumentation Lab (CIL), Guru Jambheshwar University of Science and Technology, Hisar for their instrumental support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The Synthesis route of Schiff base DFA.
(a) UV-Visible absorption spectra of DFA (3.82 × 10−5 M in methanol). (b) Absorption spectra of DFA (3.82 × 10−5 M) in different solvents.

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectral changes in ligand DFA (3.82 × 10−5 M) onto the addition of different explosives (10−5 M). (b) Specific graph to differentiate the DFA (3.82 × 10−5 M), DFA (3.82 × 10−5 M) +Picric acid (4.36 × 10−5 M) and Picric acid (4.36 × 10−5 M) only absorption bands. (c) Absorption titration spectra of DFA (3.82 × 10−5 M) with the addition of 0–1 equivalents of Picric acid.

(a) Evaluation of stoichiometric ratio of DFA (3.82 × 10−5 M) and Picric acid (4.36 × 10−5 M) by the use of Job′s plot. (b) Benesi-Hildebrand (B-H) equation for the valuation of association constant Ka, Regression constant R2 and limit of detection LOD.
Expected framework of Schiff base DFA and Picric acid adduct.
(a) 1H NMR of DFA. (b) 1H NMR of DFA-Picric acid adduct.
Bar graph representating the selectivity of Schiff base DFA for Picric acid over other explosives.

(a) Absorption spectra of Schiff base DFA (3.82 × 10−5 M) at different pH′s ranging from 2 to 11. (b) Absorption spectra of Schiff base DFA (3.82 × 10−5 M) + Picric acid (4.36 × 10−5 M) at different pH′s ranging from 2 to 11. (c) Comparative display of wavelength shift of the two solutions i.e., DFA and DFA+Picric acid at different pH's ranging from 2 to 11.

(a) Aggregation study of Schiff base DFA (3.82 × 10−5 M) in CH3OH /water as solvent mixture. (b) Aggregation study of Schiff base DFA (3.82 × 10−5 M) in CH3CN /water as solvent mixture. (c) Aggregation study of Schiff base DFA (3.82 × 10−5 M) in DMF/water as solvent mixture. (d) Aggregation study of Schiff base DFA (3.82 × 10−5 M) in THF /water as solvent mixture.

(a) FESEM image of Schiff base DFA (3.82 × 10−5 M) at low resolution. (b) FESEM image of Schiff base DFA (3.82 × 10−5 M) at high resolution to ensure thin rod like structure. (c) FESEM image of Schiff base DFA (THF)/70% water mixture solid at low resolution. (d) FESEM image of Schiff base DFA (THF)/70% water mixture solid to display uneven stone like structures.

(a) Absorption spectra of DFA (THF) and DFA (THF)/70% water mixture with or without the addition of Picric acid. (b) Optical stability evaluation of DFA and its Picric acid complex versus different reaction times in terms of absorbance.
Optimized structure of (a) Picric acid. (b) Schiff base DFA. and (c) DFA-Picric acid adduct showing bond length in Å.\\.
Energy diagram for Frontier molecular orbitals corresponding to (a) Schiff base (DFA) and (b) DFA-Picric acid adduct.
Computed harmonic vibrational frequency spectrum of Picric acid, Schiff-base (DFA), and DFA-Picric acid adduct.
Natural bond orbital (NBO) diagram of intermolecular donor-acceptor orbital′s for hydrogen bonding occurring between Schiff base (DFA) and Picric acid.
Structural parameter (Bond length) for particular bond formed during DFA-Picric acid adduct formation. The values in parenthesis show the value for isolated Schiff base DFA.

head.ce:title: Efficient detection of Picric acid by pyranone based Schiff base as a chemosensor

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Guru Jambheshwar University of Science and Technology, Hisar 125001, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guru Jambheshwar University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Guru Jambheshwar University of Science and Technology, Hisar 125001, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, Guru Jambheshwar University of Science and Technology, Hisar 125001, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guru Jambheshwar University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Keeping in mind the necessity to prepare a budget friendly chemosensor for the effortless and convenient sensing of one of the explosives i.e., Picric acid, a Schiff base 3-[-1-(4-fluoro-phenylimino)-ethyl]-4‑hydroxy-6-methyl-pyran-2-one (DFA) was synthesized by the condensation reaction between dehydroacetic and 4-fluoroaniline in ethanol solvent and characterized through analytical spectral techniques. The sensing capability of Schiff base for Picric acid as compared to other nitro explosives was examined by means of UV–Vis absorbance studies. A considerable band shift in Schiff base DFA′s absorbance band for Picric acid while irrelevant changes for the rest of explosives showed DFA′s selectivity towards Picric acid. The binding stoichiometric ratio was found to be 1:1 for DFA: Picric acid adducts which was deliberated from the job′s plot and confirmed by 1H NMR titration. The Association constant and Limit of detection were acquired to be 1.3 × 105 M−1 and 6.92 × 10−7 M respectively using Benesi-Hildebrand (B-H) equation. The sensing power of Schiff base DFA showed high selectivity and sensitivity as addition of competitive explosives showed no alteration in its detection towards Picric acid also the applicability at almost every pH and stability over an extensive time period showed DFA supremacy for Picric acid detection. DFT calculations were carried for the confirmation of theoretical structural relationships and actual absorption data. Aggregation study were too conducted in THF /water Schiff base solution and a maximum in THF /70% water solution showed aggregation at its utmost in Schiff base at 70% water solution and this was further confirmed using FESEM images.

ce:section.ce:para: In today′s world, the sensitive and selective detection of explosives is critical. Terrorist acts that have been well-coordinated throughout the world in recent years have led experts to look for better techniques to detect explosives [1]. Aside from that, rising industrial effluents are also wreaking havoc on our ecosystem, polluting land and water bodies and creating major health risks [2]. It was found that several common explosives comprise nitro compounds as key constituent. Until World War I, Trinitrotoluene was the most often used explosive among nitroaromatics [3,4]. Picric acid, on the other side, is a highly powerful explosive as compared to TNT [5]. Picric acid having phenol and nitro functional groups is highly toxic organic acid. In spite of its explosive behavior, Picric acid is a very commonly used chemical reagent and industrial compound in the manufacture of pigments, dyes, pesticides, chemical fibers, pharmaceuticals, fireworks, rocket fuel etc. [6–10]. As a result of its surplus usage and high water solubility, Picric acid readily pollutes groundwater and soil [11–13] causing significant health risks. The biodegradation products and industrial effluents of Picric acid are mutagenic and carcinogenic which fatally affect human skin, eyes and respiratory systems, and moreover it can cause chronic diseases like anemia, cyanosis and even cancer [14–17]. The utmost allowable concentration of Picric acid in water is 0.5 mg/L, according to “surface water environmental quality standards” (GB3838–2002) [18]. Therefore it seems an urgency to develop a simplistic yet efficient method for extremely sensitive and selective sensing of Picric acid in both vapor and solution phase for the sake of society safety as well as for ecological wellbeing [19].

This field is not untouched yet as there are already innumerable techniques known for Picric acid detections including latent fingerprint visualization [20], colorimetric [21], surface plasmon resonance [22], surface enhanced Raman scattering [23], electrochemistry [24], X-ray diffraction [25], fluoremetric [26], mass spectrometry [27] etc. but these approaches aren't available at common places as they call for pricey paraphernalia and experienced manpower to behold it. Moreover countless types of molecules such as nanoparticles [28], quantum dots [29], metal organic frameworks [30], conjugated polymers [31], nano composites [32], polysiloles [33], dendrimers [34], carbon nanodots [35], metal complexes [36], organic fluorophores [37], gels [38], Schiff bases [39] etc. were opted for Picric acid detection so far. Inspite of above listed techniques UV–Vis absorbance studies were opted for Picric acid detection because of its high portability, basic instrumentation, low priced personnel′s, rapid responses and easy availability [40]. Schiff bases are the class of compounds having wide range of biological activities [41] and were used as chemosesnor to detect various metal ions [42]. Here Schiff bases were choosen as chemosensor to carry out the Picric acid detection because of its too straightforward reaction mechanism, simple procedure, low pricey reactants and effortless binding accessibility to the acceptors. Herein we have synthesized a Schiff base DFA using dehydroacetic acid and 4-fluoroaniline moieties as shown in Scheme 1 and characterized it using the different analytical spectroscopic techniques such as 1H NMR, 13C NMR, FTIR and HRMS. Further DFA was effectively used for Picricacid detection via UV–Vis absorption studies and DFT calculations were too carried out to depict the results theoretically.

The key reactants Dehydroacetic acid and 4-fluoroaniline be obtained from Sigma Aldrich Pvt. Ltd. The different solvents used i.e., ethanol, acetonitirile, dimethyl sulfoxide, acetone, dichloromethane, hexane, tetrahydrofuran, dimethylformamide, diethylether, ethyl acetate, methanol, and explosives viz. phenol, toluene, benzaldehyde, 2- nitrobenzaldehyde, urea, thiourea, nitrobenzene, m- dinitrobenzene, 1‑chloro 2,4-dinitrobenzene, m-nitrophenol, 2, 4-dinitrophenol, Picricacid, dinitrotoluene, p- nitrotoulene, hydrazine were purchased from CDH and used as such without further refinement. 1H NMR was done on instrument Avance III 400 MHz Bruker with TMS as reference and CDCl3 as solvent. An FTIR spectrum was acquired via Perkin Elmer Spectrum, BX II FT-IR Spectrophotometer over a known range from 4000 to 400 cm−1 by KBR pellets. The HRMS data was taken from SCIX-QTOF Mass Spectrometer in methanol solvent. UV–VIS studies were executed within 200 to 800 nm region by using Varian Cary-5000 UV– VIS-NIR Spectrophotometer with a slit width of 2.0 nm. Systronics Digital pH Meter 335 was used to ensure the working pH range of the synthesized compound. High Resolution Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope with EDS (FE-SEM) having model no. 7610F Plus/JEOL was used for the collection of SEM images of compounds. Distilled water was used for the aggregation studies.
Schiff base 3-[−1-(4-fluoro-phenylimino)-ethyl]−4‑hydroxy-6-methyl-pyran-2-one (DFA) was synthesized as follow:

In a round bottom flask, 0.75 g (4.5 mmol) dehydroacetic acid was dissolved in 30 ml ethanol and stirred until a transparent solution was obtained. Then, ethanolic solution of 0.5 g (4.5 mmol) 4-fluoroaniline was added to the above solution followed by the continuous stirring of the mixture for 3 h and the completion of reaction was examined by TLC technique. After allowing the reaction mixture to dry overnight the solid crude was washed with ethanol and finally recrystallized the white solid using ethanol as solvent and then dried (Scheme 1). Yield: 1.092 g (87.4%), m.p.:126 ο −128 ο. 1H -NMR (400 MHz, CDCl3, TMS as internal standard): δ= 15.79 (s,1H,OH), δ= 7.19(t,2H,Haromatic ring), δ=7.17(t,2H,Haromatic ring), δ= 5.79(s,1H,Hpyranone ring), δ=2.60(s,3H,CH3), δ= 2.18(s,3H, CH3).13C NMR (100 MHz, CDCl3, 25οC, TMS) δ (ppm): 185.01(s, Cp- ring-OH), 175.73(s, C = N), 163.60 (s, Cp- ring-CH3), 163.37(s, Cp- ring=O) 160.66(s, C-F), 149.55(s, Ca-ring-N), 127.47(s, Ca-ring), 127.38(s, Ca-ring), 116.7098(s, Ca-ring-CF), 116.56(s, Ca-ring-CF), 107.14(s, Cp- ring-H), 97.56(s, Cp- ring), 20.21(s, -CH3 of p ring), and 19.95(s, -CH3). FT-IR (using KBr pellets and νmax in cm−1): 3417(νOH), 2982 (νCH, aromatic rings), 1724(νO
C
O), 1578(νC
N ). HR-MS peak at 261.98 (cal. mass m/z 261.27) with respect to [DFA]+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Designing and synthesis of Schiff base DFA
ce:section.ce:para: Schiff base DFA was synthesized via condensation of dehydroacetic acid and 4-fluoroaniline in ethanol solvent as depicted in Scheme 1. The reaction handed over us a white solid product which was further characterized by NMR, FTIR and HRMS spectrometry.1H NMR and 13C NMR spectrum [Fig. S1, S2] clearly showed peaks corresponding to the different hydrogen′s and carbons respectively as expected by the synthesized base whereas the characteristic band at 1578cm−1 analogous to C = N group gave us a comprehensible justification of DFA configuration as shown in Fig. S3. HRMS peak m/z 261.98 analogous to [M] + also supported the Schiff base formation as shown in Fig. S4. Further UV–Vis absorptions studies were opted for understanding the behavioral change of Schiff base DFA to the above listed explosives. DFA showed two intense bands at 238 nm and 322 nm as shown in Fig. 1 (a). corresponding to the п→ п* and n→п* transitions respectively.

Intially to check the solubility of DFA, solvent study with different solvents i.e., ethanol, methanol, water, dichloromethane(DCM), diethyl ether, dimethyl formamide (DMF), ethyl acetate, acetonitrile(CH3CN), tetrahydrofuran (THF) and dimethyl sulphoxide (DMSO) were carried out . It was observed from the solvent study that DFA was almost soluble in above stated solvents and showed an absorbance band in UV–Vis range in 10 ppm solution as shown in Fig. 1 (b). Herein we opted methanol instead of other solvents because most of these solvated DFA solutions does not gave a clear band in 200–270 nm range and few of them also showed negative bands which clearly helped us to choose methanol over others as solvent for further UV–Vis studies.

The different nitroaromatic and non-nitroaromatic explosive compounds i.e., urea, thiourea, toluene, paranitrotoulene, dinitrotoluene, Picric acid, benzaldehyde, m-nitrobenzaldehyde, nitrobenzene, m-dinitrobenzene, 1‑chloro 2,4 dinitrobenzene , phenol, m-nitrophenol, 2,4-dinitrophenol and hydrazine were taken to study the sensing behavior of DFA. The 1 mM stock solutions of these explosives and DFA were prepared in methanol solvent initially to conduct the absorption studies and were further diluted to prepare 3.82 × 10−5M(10 ppm) and 10−5M(10 ppm) solutions of DFA and explosives respectively. In the UV–Vis absorptions studies, DFA showed two intense bands at 238 nm and 322 nm as stated previously. Although, the addition of different explosives doesn′t leads any considerable change in DFA absorption bands except for Picric acid. It was observed in Fig. 2
(a) that on addition of Picric acid to DFA methanolic solution, the band at 322 nm showed a red shift to 337 nm (Δλ∼15 nm) and a slight blue shift was observed in 238 nm (Δλ∼6 nm) absorption band. These shifts gave us two new bands at 232 nm and 337 nm on adding Picric acid which was due to the hydrogen bonding between the two OH′s of Schiff base DFA and Picric acid respectively (Scheme 2 ) [43]. The absorption bands of DFA solution, Picric acid solution and DFA+ Picric acid solution specifically are depicted in Fig. 2 (b). The shift in 322 nm band at longer wavelength supported the expected behavior of hydrogen bonding as hydrogen bonding will increase the polarity of Schiff base and leads a red shift in its absorption spectrum.

The Picric acid methanolic solution (4.36 × 10−5 M) was added in an incremental pattern from 0.0 to 1.0 equiv. in expressions of molar ratio to the DFA (3.82 × 10−5 M) solution. Amid of these spectro-photometric titrations as shown in Fig. 2 (c), a gradual red shift in 322 nm band all along with a concurrent shrink in absorbance band intensity upto 0.5equiv was observed. Moreover, a continuous blue shift was observed in 238 nm band with a considerable hike in its absorbance intensity. The two isosbectic points at 271 nm and 337 nm ascertained the formation of a stable adduct between Picric acid and Schiff base DFA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of stiochiometric ratio: Jobs plot and association constant Ka: B-H equation
ce:section.ce:para:
The stoichiometric ratio of DFA with Picric acid was calculated using Job′s plot. For this study, methanolic solutions of DFA (3.82 × 10−5 M) was taken in 0.9 to 0.1 ml manner in separate vials to which methanolic solution of Picric acid (4.36 × 10−5 M) was added in 0.1 to 0.9 ml manner respectively such that the total volume of Schiff base DFA and Picric acid remains constant which helped us to keep the overall concentration constant inspite of varied mole fraction. From the Fig. 3
(a), the absorption maxima obtained at ∼ 0.5mole fraction indicating the 1:1 stoichiometric ratios among DFA and Picric acid (Scheme 2).

The value of Regression constant R2, Association constant Ka and LOD were analyzed by using the B-H Eq. (1) and linear curve fitting was performed by titrations of the Schiff base DFA with the Picric acid.
(1)
1 /
[
A −
A 0
]
= 1 / K a
[
A max
−
A 0
]
[
P i c r i c
a c i d
]
+ 1 /
[
A max
−
A 0
]
where A 0 is absorbance value of Schiff base DFA in lack of Picric acid, A is absorbance of particular concentration recorded in the presence of Picric acid and Amax is max. absorbance witnessed in the presence of Picric acid.

The Regression constant R2 and Association constant Ka were then found to be 97% and 1.3 × 105 M−1 correspondingly as evaluated from the linear relationship amid the 1/[A-A 0] and 1/[Picric acid] in the Benesi-Hildebrand plot in Fig. 3 (b).

LOD (limit of detection) of DFA was then computed to be 6.92 × 10−7 M using below mentioned expression; where σ is the variation from the standard value (σ=1-R2) and K is the association constant obtained from caliberation B-H curve.

Further the 1H NMR spectra of Schiff base DFA and DFA-Picric acid 1:1 adduct were taken as shown in Fig. 4
(a) and (b). Here Fig. 4 (b) clearly showed the effect of hydrogen bonding between the two OH′s of Schiff base DFA and Picric acid as there observed a visible chemical shift in the OH peak of Schiff base DFA onto the adduct formation from 15.79 ppm to 15.29 ppm. Similarly the proton of OH group of Picric acid was shifted from 5.35 ppm (evaluated from the used Picric acid 1H NMR) to 4.82 ppm. These NMR spectra helped us to validate the 1:1 stiochiometry ratio among the DFA and Picric acid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Competitive experimentation for validation of effectiveness of Schiff base
ce:section.ce:para: Herein for this experimentation, Schiff base DFA (3.82 × 10−5 M) was taken in 1:1 equiv. with different methanolic solutions of explosives i.e., urea, thiourea, toluene, paranitrotoulene, dinitrotoluene, benzaldehyde, m-nitrobenzaldehyde, nitrobenzene, m-dinitrobenzene, 1‑chloro 2, 4 dinitrobenzene , phenol, m-nitrophenol, 2, 4-dinitrophenol and hydrazine (10−5 M) separately and further UV–Vis spectra were obtained for these different solutions. The absorption data of these solutions showed no considerable band shift in DFA absorption band in the absence of Picric acid whereas further addition of Picric acid to each of above mentioned solutions leads the band shift of Picric acid in the different solutions. This data pointed out that the existence of rest of intrusive explosives barely affects the Picric acid detection as shown in Fig. 5 . and thus this experiment became an evidence of DFA′s selectivity and specificity towards Picric acid over other explosives.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH effect on Picric acid detection
ce:section.ce:para:
Generally, there observed a variation in detection process of ions, anions and explosives onto the deviation in pH of the initial solutions. It was previously noted that a slight variation in the recognition environment whether it was acidic or alkaline, can induce the C = N fracture which will further affect the optical properties and recognition ability of these compounds. Here, absorbance of different pH solutions of Schiff base DFA ranging from 2 to 11 which were prepared initially using HCl and NaOH solutions were examined as shown in Fig. 6
(a). This Fig. 6 (a) concluded that DFA had no variation in its absorption band onto the alteration in pH of its solution. Afterwards when Picric acid was added to the above mentioned different pH solutions, a considerable band shifts in different pH solutions were observed. As shown in Fig. 6 (b), there monitored a slight yet considerable band shift in the acidic medium whereas a significant band shift was noticed in neutral and basic medium. The lesser sensitivity in acidic medium was may be due to the presence of excessive H + in the solution which can create hindrance in hydrogen bonding of Schiff base DFA and Picric acid. Further Fig. 6 (c) depicted the whole data in terms of wavelength shifts and helped us to conclude that Schiff base DFA showed best detection of Picric acid at every pH but increase in basicity of the solution leads its bestest detection. However, DFA showed utmost band shift for the working pH ∼7.8, that's why the rest of the studies were done using the original pH Schiff base solution.

The AIAE study indicates the enhancement in UV–Vis absorption intensity of Schiff base in the presence of water which tends the aggregation of Schiff base. The aggregation study of Schiff base DFA (3.82 × 10−5 M) was carried out in various solvents i.e., methanol (CH3OH) /water, dimethyl formamide (DMF) /water, acetonitrile (CH3CN) /water and tetrahydrofuran (THF) /water to check the aggregation behavior of Schiff base DFA towards different solvents. For this purpose, the list of binary mixtures of Schiff base DFA were prepared by increasing 10% water, 20% water, 30% water,……………….,70% water, 80% water, 90% water content respectively in the different solvated DFA solutions and then sonicated for 20 min. It was observed that Schiff base DFA had neither visible change in vials nor showed any enhancement in absorption intensity in CH3OH /water, DMF/water, and CH3CN /water mixtures as shown in Fig. 7
(a), (b), (c) which means that there occured no aggregation of DFA molecules in the presence of water. However in THF /water (Fig. 7 (d)). Schiff base solution, there observed a continuous blue shift (Δλ∼1–17 nm) onto the increment of water content along with slight precipitates in the mixtures and maximum band shift and precipitates obtained were for 70% water mixture. These band shifts were accompanied by the variation in absorption intensity of Schiff base where enhancements in intensities were only observed in 60%, 70% and 80% water mixtures. A maximum at 70% water content showed AIAE at its utmost in case of THF /water Schiff base solution which gave us the confirmation of aggregation state in Schiff base at 70% whereas further reduction in absorption intensities in 80% and 90% mixtures were may be due to rapture in aggregates of Schiff base DFA.

To ensure the formation of aggregates, FESEM measurements were performed for Schiff base DFA (THF) and DFA (THF)/70% water mixture. For this study the solid DFA was taken as such and white precipitated DFA (THF)/70% water mixture was placed on a glass plate to evacuate its moisture, the two solids were then subjected to the instrument to get the FE-SEM images. As shown in Fig. 8
(a), (b) DFA has very clear thin rod like structure whereas aggregation can be clearly noted by Fig. 8 (c), (d) where DFA (THF)/70% water mixture displayed uneven stone like structures.

After this it is very interesting to know that when the DFA (THF) solution was tested in UV–Vis spectroscopy, a very little shift in DFA (THF) 327 nm band was observed onto the addition of Picric acid but the DFA (THF)/70% water mixture 305 nm band gave a significant band shift for Picric acid by giving a new band at 334 nm as shown in Fig. 9
(a). Further studies were not carried out for multiple reasons as in 200–270 nm range a negative absorption band was observed also absorbance greater than 1 is not generally considerable.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Time of response and optical stability
ce:section.ce:para: The term time of response here mentioned the time taken by Schiff base DFA in the sensing of Picric acid which further can help us in considering its necessity in environmental and biological samples evaluation. A fast response time of ∼40 s (at fast scan rate) was observed in DFA to detect Picric acid. For the valuation of optical stability of Schiff base DFA and its Picric acid complex, the absorbance intensity of these two solutions were computed at regular intervals and the results amazed us because of no amendment in the absorbance intensities as shown in Fig. 9 (b). Also the absorbance of synthesized Schiff base was tested even after 15 days which showed no alteration in its absorption intensities. This observation encouraged us for using DFA for long term accessibility.

To get an enhanced access to structural property relationship between Schiff base DFA and its Picric acid adduct, DFT calculations were executed on Gaussian 09 software by using B3LYP hybrid functional which uses correction for both exchange and correlation [44]. The basis sets chosen for describing the atomic orbitals of C, N, O of both Schiff base DFA and Picric acid was 6–311++G(d,p). The optimized structure for Picric acid, Schiff base DFA and DFA-Picric acid adduct were deliberated in Fig. 10
(a), (b), and (c) respectively. Now Fig. 10 (c) and Table 1 showed the bond distances between relevant atoms where there is possibility of H-bonding in an adduct [45]. It is observed that the bond distance of hydroxyl group (OH) of Picric acid and Schiff base DFA increases during adduct formation. Here Table 1 showed that the bond is formed between H and O atom which corresponds to inter-molecular H-Bonding between Picric acid and Schiff base DFA.

Further the frontier molecular orbitals (FMO′s) of Schiff base DFA and DFA-Picric acid adduct are depicted in Fig. 11
(a) and (b) to understand the possible energy transfer. In case of Schiff base DFA, HOMO has the electron concentration mainly due to the pyranone framework of Schiff base which showed the possible binding with Picric acid whereas LOMO′s electron concentration is positioned over the entire molecule. The FMO of DFA-Picric acid adduct showed different electron concentration from the Schiff base DFA orbitals as here it mainly resides on Picric acid moiety. This shift in the electron concentration leads the possible intermolecular- hydrogen bonding among the Schiff base DFA and Picric acid moiety [43]. The difference in energy between frontier molecular orbitals of Schiff base DFA and DFA-Picric acid adduct were found to be 4.15 eV and 3.56 eV respectively. This diminish in the energy gap suggested that there is some energy transfer or favorable interaction occurred between the two moieties and hence showed a good conformity with experimental results. Also, we have calculated the stabilization energy of adduct formed between Schiff base DFA and Picric acid. The stabilization energy of adduct was calculated using the following equation:
E
S t a b
=
E
a d d u c t
−
(
E
D F A
+
E
P i c r i c
a c i d
)
where, Estab is the stabilization energy for the adduct formation, Eadduct, EDFA, EPicric acid are the energies of isolated adduct, DFA, and Picric acid, respectively.

The negative value of stabilization energy indicates that the adduct formed is stable. The stabilization energy of adduct was found to be −29 kJ/mol which shows that adduct is stable [46]. Further, we have theoretically characterized the isolated Picric acid, Schiff base DFA, and their adduct by calculating the harmonic vibrational spectra (Fig. 12 ) and UV–visible spectra (Figure S5). The harmonic vibrational spectra showed change in the peak position of hydroxyl group. During adduct formation, the OH peak shifts towards lower frequency which is characteristic for inter-molecular H-bonding. Also, the UV-absorption peak shifts towards higher wavelength i.e. bathochromic shift, when adduct was formed between Picric acid and Schiff base DFA. The above results were in conformity with the experimental results.

Further the optimized geometries were subjected to analyze the charge transfer by inter and intra molecular delocalization energy using the natural bond orbitals (NBO) (see Fig. 13 ). The donor-acceptor orbital interactions are assessed by probing all possible Lewis and non-Lewis NBO′s by second-order perturbation theory. Higher the stabilization energy E(2) higher will be the delocalization from the filled Lewis NBO′s into the unoccupied non-Lewis orbital′s. All the NBO calculations were performed by employing the NBO 3.1 program incorporated in the Gaussian09 software. For rendering and visulation of optimized geometries of complexes, GaussView and Chemcraft were used.

Natural bond orbitals (NBO) are used to study inter- and intra-molecular hybridization and delocalization of electrons. To study the inter- and intra-molecular hydrogen-bond interaction between Schiff base (DFA) and Picric acid, donor-acceptor orbitals were generated and analyzed (see Fig. 13). The NBO diagram clearly indicated that one of the O(40)-H(44) bond acts as donor and other O(9)-H(10) acts as acceptor . Due to these, the electron density has been transferred from donor to the acceptor orbitals and thus produced stabilization energy of −29 kJ/mol. Also, the NO2 group of Picric acid and OH group of DFA forms the intermolecular hydrogen bonding. Therefore, we can say that the DFA-Picric acid adduct was stabilized due to H-Bonding.

We have designed and characterized a pyranone funtionalized Schiff base DFA for the cost effective, quirky and handy detection of Picric acid derived from dehydroacetic and 4-fluoroaniline. DFA showed easy solubility in almost every accessible solvent. Furthermore in terms of work, DFA showed a considerable band shifts in the two UV–Vis absorption bands prior for Picric acid rather than rest of explosives. Interestingly, the hydrogen bonding between two OH′s of Schiff base and Picric acid leads the formation of 1:1 adduct between DFA: Picric acid which was confirmed by Jobs plot. DFT calculations also validated the analysis results by the studies (FMO diagram, stabilization energy calculation and NBO calculations) and different characterizations i.e., vibrational frequency spectra and theoretical UV–Vis spectra. The reported Schiff base DFA offered a 97% Regression constant R2, 1.3 × 105 M−1 Association constant Ka and LOD of 6.92 × 10−7 M by using linear relationship in the Benesi-Hildebrand plot. DFA also showed maximum aggregation in THF /70% water solvent which was easily observable due to enhancement in the DFA absorbance in THF solvent. The formation of aggregates was also confirmed by FESEM images. Schiff base DFA has numerous advantages in terms of budget friendly and easy synthesis procedure, low LOD at handy instrument, no distraction in absorbance at different pH, solubility in lab available solvents, stability over a range of time period and sensing of desired Picric acid without any relevant interference of other explosives concluded it to be the right decision to opt for Picric acid detection.
Apurva Goel designed and synthesized the methodology and carried out the whole analysis and writing of original draft.
Rajesh Malhotra contributed as being the supervisor of this research work reviewed and edited the paper.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Apurva Goel is pleased to CSIR, New Delhi, for pecuniary assistance in terms of JRF fellowship with CSIR sanction letter No. 09/752(0070)/2017-EMR-I. The authors are grateful to the Central Instrumentation Lab (CIL), Guru Jambheshwar University of Science and Technology, Hisar for their instrumental support.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.molstruc.2021.131619.
